caveats: a: OEWS combines all NAICS 311800 segments and excludes self-employed workers.
a: The FDA cookie examples are illustrative regulatory guidance, not a representative time study.
a: The mapping is judgmental and cannot precisely isolate tasks already automated.
rho: No 311821-specific five-year AI implementation cohort was located.
rho: Large branded firms have richer data and integration capacity than many LMM private-label or regional manufacturers.
e: Mondelēz is global and far larger than the target band, so it demonstrates channels rather than the eligible share.
e: The provided target-band firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms with unusual brand economics or capital intensity.
s5: No source directly measures qualifying five-year control transfers for eligible target-band cookie and cracker manufacturers.
s5: Private strategic acquisitions and distressed plant sales can be difficult to distinguish in public records.
q: Mondelēz has unusually strong brands and global scale, which may increase pricing power relative to target firms, while its large retailers may exert greater bargaining power.
q: Observed input-cost pricing elasticity is not a direct measure of pass-through of AI-enabled labor savings.
d5: BLS output is four-digit and includes multiple bakery and tortilla categories.
d5: Mondelēz's North American result includes biscuits and baked snacks and reflects one large branded portfolio rather than the full U.S. industry.
o: Persistence of operator-required demand does not guarantee persistence of independent target firms; larger manufacturers or retailer-owned brands may take share.
o: The estimate separates operator displacement from ordinary category demand loss, which is captured in d5.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat wholesale product supply priced per unit, case, shipment, negotiated retail program, private-label contract, or co-manufacturing arrangement, often accompanied by discounts, rebates, volume incentives, displays, and other trade promotions.
lens.excluded_activities: Immediate-consumption cookie shops, baked-goods retailers, pretzel manufacturing, pasta and purchased-flour mix manufacturing, captive internal plants without external customers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market manufacturers of cookies, crackers, ice cream cones, and similar products that repeatedly supply external supermarkets, discounters, warehouse clubs, food distributors, foodservice customers, private-label owners, or contract-manufacturing customers.

a.bridge: BLS OEWS measures the broader four-digit NAICS 311800 population rather than cookie and cracker manufacturers alone. I mapped forecasting, production planning, purchasing, inventory, quality-document review, customer and retailer administration, sales analysis, marketing, and management as substantially AI-exposed; physical mixing, depositing, baking, packing, sanitation, maintenance, warehousing, and transport as less exposed; then judgmentally wage-weighted those groups. Cookie and cracker plants may be more automated and shelf-stable than the broader mix, likely lowering unautomated production exposure but increasing data-rich commercial and inventory workflows.
caveats: OEWS combines all NAICS 311800 segments and excludes self-employed workers.
The FDA cookie examples are illustrative regulatory guidance, not a representative time study.
The mapping is judgmental and cannot precisely isolate tasks already automated.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 311821 payroll and task-time study split by plant automation and business model, measuring commercial planning, promotion analytics, scheduling, procurement, quality records, customer administration, maintenance planning, and line tasks, would materially move the estimate.
a.rationale: Broader bakery employment is dominated by production at 49.70% and transportation and material moving at 13.23%, while management, business operations, sales, office support, planning, and scientific quality work form a smaller but higher-wage exposed layer. FDA cookie-process examples show automated monitoring coexisting with manual checks, records, QC review, investigation, and corrective-action work that AI can assist but not wholly remove.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for broader NAICS 311800 from 62.1 billion chained 2017 dollars in 2024 to 74.4 billion in 2034, implying a five-year compound ratio near 1.094. I center 311821 lower at 1.05 because Mondelēz reported North American biscuits and baked-snacks category softness and unfavorable volume/mix linked to pricing elasticity, while allowing upside from convenience, innovation, and population growth. The four-digit product mix and the single-company signal can bias the estimate in either direction.
caveats: BLS output is four-digit and includes multiple bakery and tortilla categories.
Mondelēz's North American result includes biscuits and baked snacks and reflects one large branded portfolio rather than the full U.S. industry.
d5.change_evidence: Six-digit 311821 real shipments and scanner unit data for cookies, crackers, cones, branded, private-label, foodservice, and co-manufacturing channels, adjusted for pack size and quality mix, would materially narrow the range.
d5.rationale: Cookies and crackers are shelf-stable convenience foods with broad retail distribution, but volumes are exposed to health preferences, discretionary spending, pricing elasticity, brand/private-label substitution, and category maturity. Moderate real growth remains plausible, but contraction cannot be ruled out over five years.

caveats: Mondelēz is global and far larger than the target band, so it demonstrates channels rather than the eligible share.
The provided target-band firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms with unusual brand economics or capital intensity.
e.change_evidence: A representative LMM 311821 sample with customer invoice recurrence, customer tenure, affiliate sales, private-label and co-manufacturing terms, and seasonal-program dependence would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: Manufacturers normally serve repeat downstream retail, wholesale, foodservice, private-label, or brand customers. Large-company evidence confirms recurring supermarket, discounter, digital-commerce, warehouse-club, and distributor relationships and trade-promotion programs. A minority may be captive, overly dependent on one-off seasonal programs, or lack a transferable recurring external-customer base.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311821 ($2,284,728,666) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311821 ($9,877,283,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $312,256 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311821); class <5: 143 firms, avg revenue/firm $458,769 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $69,962 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 43 firms, avg revenue/firm $658,767 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $100,462 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 46 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,557,739 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $390,055 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 96 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,798,573 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,036,782 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $47,629,400 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,263,484 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $355,559,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $54,222,748 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 121

caveats: Persistence of operator-required demand does not guarantee persistence of independent target firms; larger manufacturers or retailer-owned brands may take share.
The estimate separates operator displacement from ordinary category demand loss, which is captured in d5.
o.change_evidence: Retailer and brand-owner procurement data measuring shifts from outsourced U.S. cookie and cracker manufacturers to in-house production, imports, direct-to-consumer microproduction, or non-bakery snacks would test operator displacement.
o.rationale: Cookies and crackers are physical packaged foods requiring manufacturing, baking, packaging, allergen control, quality release, warehousing, and distribution. Software can optimize and coordinate supply but cannot replace the edible product, and applicable food rules preserve accountable manufacturing and labeling obligations. Limited displacement can come from customer insourcing, imports, or substitution to other snacks rather than software supply.

caveats: Mondelēz has unusually strong brands and global scale, which may increase pricing power relative to target firms, while its large retailers may exert greater bargaining power.
Observed input-cost pricing elasticity is not a direct measure of pass-through of AI-enabled labor savings.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract and SKU-level gross-margin histories around labor-saving deployments, including rebates, promotional accruals, price, volume, shelf space, and bid outcomes segmented by branded, private-label, co-manufacturing, and foodservice channels, would reveal realized retention.
q.rationale: Product pricing provides no automatic labor-cost pass-through, but consolidated retailers can demand lower prices, more promotions, longer terms, or greater trade spending, while private-label alternatives discipline branded pricing. Mondelēz records discounts, rebates, coupons, volume incentives, displays, and trade programs as revenue reductions and reported volume/mix pressure from pricing elasticity, demonstrating multiple benefit-sharing channels. Strong differentiated brands or specialized formulations should retain more than commodity private-label suppliers.

caveats: No 311821-specific five-year AI implementation cohort was located.
Large branded firms have richer data and integration capacity than many LMM private-label or regional manufacturers.
rho.change_evidence: A multi-plant cohort comparing baseline and post-deployment labor hours, forecast error, promotion accuracy, waste, service levels, quality deviations, allergen incidents, and audit findings for twelve to twenty-four months would directly test implementation.
rho.rationale: Shelf-stable products, repeated production runs, retailer data, promotion histories, and structured quality records create favorable implementation conditions for forecasting, scheduling, promotion analysis, procurement, document review, and exception handling. ERP integration, customer data rights, food-safety validation, allergen controls, and human accountability limit autonomous deployment.

caveats: No source directly measures qualifying five-year control transfers for eligible target-band cookie and cracker manufacturers.
Private strategic acquisitions and distressed plant sales can be difficult to distinguish in public records.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of PitchBook, CapIQ, GF Data, Axial, SBA 7(a), and food-industry adviser transactions tagged to 311821, screened for going-concern control transfers and matched to eligible firms, would materially refine the probability.
s5.rationale: Regional, specialty, private-label, and family-owned manufacturers can attract strategic or sponsor buyers, but food-safety systems, retailer approvals, plant condition, brand value, and customer concentration narrow the transferable set. The probability excludes closures, asset liquidations, internal reorganizations, and family transfers without control change.

principal_driver: Repeat retailer and distributor replenishment creates durable customer relationships and rich planning data.
principal_weakness: Mechanized physical production limits AI-addressable labor, while retailers and promotions can recapture much of the benefit.
